role_summary: First SNCC field secretary and SNCC director for southwest Georgia (1961); co-founder of the Albany Movement; co-founder (with wife Shirley Sherrod) of New Communities Inc, the 1969 collective farm modeled on Israeli kibbutzim that pioneered the U.S. community-land-trust movement; Albany City Council member 1976-1990.
ai_reading: Sherrod's neighbor counts are modest (peak 31), suggesting a comparatively shorter or more topically-focused interview transcript than most catalog entries. His top neighbors are C. T. Vivian (entry 134, SCLC direct-action teacher), Charles McLaurin (entry 17, Mississippi SNCC), William G. Anderson (entry 126, Albany Movement co-founder), Annie Pearl Avery (entry 3, SNCC voter-registration), and James Oscar Jones (entry 57). The Anderson cosine is the catalog's most direct same-movement co-founder pairing (both were founders of the Albany Movement; Anderson was its first president, Sherrod a SNCC field director within it). On the Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision axis Sherrod sits at +0.67, well toward Strategic Vision, and at +0.59 toward Armed Self-Defense on the nonviolence axis, both readings consistent with the Wyatt Tee Walker (entry 132) pattern of SCLC-and-Albany-Movement figures landing on the Strategic + Self-Defense side of the catalog's two AI-vs-culture-inverted axes despite their canonical nonviolent-leadership reception.
biographical_paragraph: Sherrod joined SNCC in 1961, becoming the organization's first field secretary and its director for southwest Georgia; he was among the four students who chose jail-no-bail in Rock Hill, South Carolina that year, the tactical commitment that pulled SNCC's full-time staff cadre out of college [src: 2]. He relocated to Albany, Georgia in 1961 and, with fellow SNCC member Cordell Reagon, organized the direct-action campaigns that became the Albany Movement; in 1963 the City of Albany repealed all of its segregation ordinances [src: 2]. In 1969 Sherrod and his wife Shirley co-founded New Communities Inc, a collective farm in southwest Georgia modeled on Israeli kibbutzim that pioneered the U.S. community-land-trust movement [src: 2]. He served on the Albany City Council from 1976 through 1990, was later a prison chaplain, and taught at Albany State University; born January 2, 1937 in Surry, Virginia, he died October 11, 2022 in Albany, Georgia, at eighty-five [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 18 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

sources.title: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Charles Sherrod, Civil Rights Leader in Albany, Ga., Dies at 85
